closed_children.title: SPEC: XDG layout and orphan-branch bootstrap
Rewrite SPEC-workspace-layout under the three-file model + delivery/pre_check rename
SPEC-clone-layout: resolve open questions surfaced while implementing Phase 1
Phase 1: dual-read XDG layout, bl init writes XDG only
Phase 1A: Store::discover XDG-first with legacy dual-read

description:
Pivot the project layout so that balls touches **nothing** on the code branch (no committed config, no .gitignore insertion, no .balls-worktrees colocated in-tree). All workspace artifacts — config, state-repo clones, worktrees, plugin auth, claims, locks — relocate under XDG dirs in the user's home. Bootstrap stops being a file path and becomes a well-known orphan branch name on origin.

## Why

The original design intent was that bl is invisible from the code repo's perspective — clone the repo without bl installed, see nothing balls-shaped. The current shape diverges:

- `.balls/config.json` is committed on main (bl-e609 deliberately put it there).
- `.balls-worktrees/` colocates inside the repo, requiring a gitignore entry.
- The "what's tracked vs ignored under .balls/" mental model has to be carried by hand (state-repo gitignored, config.json tracked, project.json symlinked, plugin auth ignored).

A maintainer-side re-examination concluded the current architecture is a stepping stone, not the destination. bl-8a9a unified the state checkout (retiring federated mode), bl-e609 split workspace/project config — both correct given their predecessors, both necessary scaffolding for the move this epic completes. Sequencing: yes, we're pivoting partway through. The earlier balls aren't wrong, they're earlier.

## The target shape

- Bootstrap fact = the well-known branch name. bl compiled knowing 'origin balls/tasks' is always the first hop. To go anywhere else (federation, alternate branch name, multi-project per repo), origin balls/tasks carries a project.json with a redirect — state_url / state_branch fields. One mechanism, no modes.
- Workspace-owned config moves from the code branch to the workspace's own origin balls/tasks branch (alongside the redirect). bl-e609's split is preserved — workspace fields vs project fields — but workspace fields now ride a different orphan branch (own) instead of a real file on main.
- Everything else lives under XDG paths keyed by workspace identity: ~/.config/balls/<key>/, ~/.local/state/balls/<key>/, ~/.cache/balls/<key>/.
- Worktrees relocate to ~/.local/state/balls/<key>/worktrees/<id>/. Colocation property is lost; pristine-main property is gained. The trade-off was named and accepted.
- Workspace identity is keyed by git remote URL (origin.url). Two clones of the same origin share state-repo cache + tracker access; per-clone subdir under <key> isolates claims/locks/worktrees so concurrent clones don't collide. Same scope as the current 'workspace-owned = per-code-repo' semantics, just relocated.

## What this resolves

- Original vision (no balls footprint on code branch) finally holds.
- Plugin auth lives in a directory that's *entirely* local — no ignored/unignored weave.
- 'Bootstrap fact must be readable before anything else' becomes a branch-name convention compiled into bl, not a file-location convention. Trackers still cannot redirect a workspace (the branch name is fixed; only the redirect contents are tracker-owned). Principle 4 of SPEC-tracker-state survives in stronger form.

## Non-goals (preserve from SPEC-tracker-state §14)

- Per-workspace overrides of project-owned fields. Federation is still per-code-repo, not per-clone.
- N-way tracker reconciliation. Single-active throughout. The 'list of trackers' alternative was considered and rejected; this is the single-tracker model with the address relocated.
- Cross-tracker task movement. Still detach-then-remaster.

## Phasing

The migration is breaking but stages cleanly with no history rewrites. Phases are intentionally individually-shippable so we can stop at any layer:

0. SPEC rewrite (first child of this epic).
1. Dual-read with preference for new — bl reads both old layout and new, prefers new where present. No user-visible change for existing repos. Safety net for everything after.
2. bl migrate command — idempotent, refuses-if-dirty, moves state-repo + worktrees + config in one shot, commits one final 'balls: migrate to XDG layout' on main, removes .balls/ and the gitignore entries. That commit is the last thing balls ever writes on main.
3. bl prime auto-detect + warning on legacy layout; later, --migrate becomes default.
4. min_bl_version bump on the orphan branch project.json, after teams have upgraded.

## The accepted cost

Old bl on a migrated repo silently misroutes (operates on local origin balls/tasks instead of the federated tracker). Same documented-but-not-engineered caveat as SPEC-tracker-state §12. Mitigation is min_bl_version + social coordination (everyone upgrades before Phase 2 lands); no force-push mitigation is possible. For solo work, irrelevant.

## Why an epic and not one ball

The SPEC depends on architectural decisions that should land separately from implementation. Dual-read is a safety net that has independent value even if later phases stall. Migration code is large enough to warrant its own ball. min_bl_version is a coordination call that only makes sense once Phase 1/2 are deployed. Each child carries its own conformance tests; bl-8a9a → bl-e609 demonstrated this works.
title: Relocate balls off the code branch — XDG layout, orphan-branch bootstrap
